title: Kindness
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: What Is Kindness?
subpoints: The inclination of the heart that seeks the welfare of others
Flows from selflessness, not selfish ambition (Philippians 2:3-4)
A gospel grace: given to those who do not deserve it, based on compassion not merit
In the New Testament, kindness often appears in the context of difficulties

outline.title: Kindness as an Attribute of God
subpoints: God's loving-kindness (hesed) — steadfast love, mercy, faithfulness — is the richest OT word for kindness
Isaiah pluralizes kindness: 'the lovingkindnesses of the Lord' (Isaiah 63:7)
We are to be kind because God is kind even to the ungrateful and evil (Luke 6:35-36)
The incarnation is the physical manifestation of kindness: 'When the kindness... of God appeared, He saved us' (Titus 3:4-5)

outline.title: Three Examples of Christ's Kindness
subpoints: Kindly feeding the multitude (Mark 6:30-44): Jesus set aside his need for rest to teach and feed the crowd
Kindly healing the leper (Matthew 8:1-4): Jesus touched an untouchable and said 'I am willing'
Kindly forgiving from the cross (Luke 23:34): 'Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they do'

outline.title: Kindness Is Not Natural — But We Are Free
subpoints: Romans 3:10 — 'There is none who does kindness, no not one' — it is against our fallen nature
But Christ has set us free from self-reliance (Galatians 5:1)
Knowing the kindness of God enables us to show the kindness of God

outline.title: The Power of Kindness as Witness
subpoints: Tertullian: pagans recognized Christians for acts of charity and kindness
The Greek word for kindness (chrestotes) sounds like Christos — early believers were called 'kindness people'
Augustine: it was Ambrose's kindness, not his eloquence, that drew him toward Christ
Promise: God will show 'the exceeding riches of His grace in His kindness toward us in Christ Jesus' (Ephesians 2:7)

summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches on kindness as the fifth fruit of the Spirit, working from Colossians 3:12-17. He defines kindness as the inclination of the heart to seek the welfare of others — a selfless, gospel-rooted grace that cannot be earned or merited. Kindness is established first as an attribute of God, traced through the Old Testament word hesed (loving-kindness) and culminating in the incarnation: when Christ appeared, the very kindness of God took on flesh and walked among us (Titus 3:4-5). Three vivid scenes from Jesus' life are examined as guides to kindness in practice: feeding the multitude despite his own exhaustion (Mark 6), touching and healing an untouchable leper (Matthew 8), and praying for his executioners from the cross (Luke 23). Pastor Lyro then confronts the hard truth that kindness is not natural to fallen humanity (Romans 3:10) but insists that those united to Christ have been freed from self-centeredness to genuinely care for others. The sermon closes with the remarkable historical testimony that the early pagan world recognized Christians precisely by their acts of kindness — calling them "kindness people" — and with Augustine's example that it was Ambrose's personal kindness that opened the door to the gospel.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Colossians 3:12-17

If you're able, you may stand as we read God's Word. We turn to Colossians chapter 3. We read verses 12 through 17 as we continue our series on the fruit of the Spirit. This is the Word of our Lord:

"Therefore, as the elect of God, holy and beloved, put on tender mercies, kindness, humility, meekness, long-suffering, bearing with one another and forgiving one another. If anyone has a complaint against another, even as Christ forgave you, so you also must do. But above all these things put on love, which is the bond of perfection, and let the peace of God rule in your hearts, to which also you were called in one body; and be thankful. Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly in all wisdom, teaching and admonishing one another in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing with grace in your hearts to the Lord. And whatever you do in word or deed, do all in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through Him."

## Introduction

We are continuing our series on the fruit of the Spirit. Galatians 5:22-23 says: "The fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, long-suffering, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, self-control." Today to love, joy, peace, and patience we add kindness.

I was tempted to combine kindness and goodness in one sermon, because they often cross into each other's territory. The word translated kindness in Galatians 5:22 is often elsewhere translated goodness. For example, in Romans 11:22 — "Consider the goodness and severity of God" — that word goodness is the same word as kindness. But we will profit immensely from separate sermons on each.

## What Is Kindness?

Kindness is the inclination of the heart that seeks the welfare of others. The idea of kindness is included in Philippians 2:3-4, where Paul exhorts the church to humility: "Let nothing be done through selfish ambition or conceit, but in lowliness of mind let each esteem others better than himself. Let each of you look out not only for his own interests, but also for the interests of others."

Kindness flows from selflessness. Kindness considers the needs of others. We live out kindness when we look to the interest and well-being of others independently of any service in return. When we behave in a kind way to others expecting to get something back from them, that is not kindness at all — that is self-centeredness. Kindness is to do good to others for the sake of doing good to others.

Kindness is a gospel grace. Like the gospel, it is not given to those who deserve it. Kindness is based on compassionate care, not merit. And when we look at what the New Testament says about the practice of kindness, we find that it comes precisely in the context of difficulties.

## Kindness as an Attribute of God

Remember how we saw that patience was an attribute of God last week, that peace is an attribute of God the week before, and that joy and love are attributes of God? Guess where we are going next. Kindness is an attribute of God.

In Ephesians 4:32, the Apostle says: "Be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, even as God in Christ forgave you." The pattern of kindness is God in Christ.

In Luke 6:35-36, the Lord Jesus says: "Love your enemies, do good, and lend, hoping for nothing in return; and your reward will be great, and you will be sons of the Most High. For He is kind to the unthankful and evil. Therefore be merciful, just as your Father also is merciful."

The Old Testament authors describe God as kind by using the very rich Hebrew word hesed. This word is so deep in meaning that it is translated many different ways — sometimes steadfast love, sometimes faithfulness, sometimes mercy. My favorite translation is how the Old King James translated it: "loving-kindness." Psalm 36:7: "How excellent is your lovingkindness, O God!" Psalm 63:3: "Your lovingkindness is better than life." Isaiah goes further and pluralizes this word — grammatically incorrect but inspired: "I will mention the lovingkindnesses of the Lord" (Isaiah 63:7) — spoken to a rebellious nation, showing that God deals with His people according to His kindness.

From these passages we learn that kindness is a key component of the character of God. There is no conception of God apart from kindness. God is not just kind — God is kindness. And we can go further: God is kindness with a capital K, because Jesus Christ is kindness incarnate.

In Titus 3:4-5, Paul encourages Titus, who was pastoring difficult people on Crete: "When the kindness and the love of God our Savior toward man appeared, not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to His mercy He saved us." When Paul speaks of the most awesome moment in all of history — when heaven came down to earth and God took upon Himself a human nature — he simply says: the kindness of God appeared. The incarnation is the physical, tangible manifestation of kindness. When Mary wrapped her child in swaddling cloth, she was wrapping kindness in flesh.

## Three Examples of Christ's Kindness

Because Jesus is kindness incarnate, He is our best guide to kindness. I want to give you three examples.

**First: Jesus kindly feeds the multitude (Mark 6:30-44).** This episode begins with Jesus seeking rest after exhausting ministry. He and the disciples go to a deserted place — hoping for some rest. And then what happens? The people follow him. What is his response? He had compassion on the crowd because they were "like sheep not having a shepherd" (v. 34). He was moved to kindness and kindness always moves us toward doing something about other people's needs. He teaches them first — because He realized that people's spiritual need to hear the gospel is greater than His need for rest. Then He feeds them miraculously until they were satisfied. This is the only miracle found in all four Gospels. And it is a miracle about Jesus' kindness.

**Second: Jesus kindly heals a leper (Matthew 8:1-4).** Leprosy made one ceremonially unclean and permanently barred from the temple. But this leper knew Jesus would not turn him away. Notice two things Jesus does: He touches him — a man who likely had not felt human touch for a long time. And He says, "I am willing" — not being compelled, but desiring to help. Real kindness desires the welfare of others. Kindness sees the needs and meets the needs. And notice: if anyone else had touched this leper, they would have become unclean. But Jesus, who is holiness itself, cleanses the man instead of becoming unclean Himself.

**Third: Jesus kindly forgives from the cross (Luke 23:34).** As He takes His last breath, Jesus says: "Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they do." In response to mob, injustice, hatred, and violence, Jesus prays with His last breath for the forgiveness of the very people who had done all of this to Him.

In Romans 2:4, Paul says: "Do you despise the riches of His goodness" — that word goodness is our word for kindness — "forbearance and long-suffering, not knowing that the goodness of God leads you to repentance?" It is the kindness of God that leads us to repentance.

## Kindness Is Not Natural — But We Are Free

Brothers and sisters, kindness is not natural to us. If kindness were natural to us, it would not need to be a fruit of the Spirit. In Romans 3:10, in a statement we sometimes miss because of translation: "There is none who does good" — that word good is the word for kindness — "no, not one." There is no one who does kindness naturally.

But you and I are not bound to act according to what is natural to fallen humanity, because we have been freed. "Stand fast therefore in the liberty by which Christ has made us free" (Galatians 5:1). You are free to not care about yourself. That is one of the greatest freedoms we can experience — to not care about ourselves and to care instead about Christ, God, and others.

We have experienced the kindness of Jesus in our lives. He has met all our needs and feeds our souls. He reached out and touched us when we were repulsive. He has forgiven us all our sins. Knowing the kindness of God enables us to show the kindness of God.

## The Power of Kindness as Witness

Don't underestimate kindness. Kindness is one of the most powerful tools we have to bear witness to Christ.

Tertullian, a second- and third-century pastor, tells us that Christians were often recognized by the pagan world for their acts of charity and kindness. The Greek word for kindness in Galatians 5:22 is chrestotes. Tertullian tells us of a pun that developed: Christians are called Christiani, but the world began calling them Chrestiani — "kindness people." Not followers of Christ, but kindness people.

About a hundred years later, Augustine — one of the most influential theologians of all time — tells us that what brought him closer to Christ was the kindness that Ambrose, the bishop of Milan, showed to him. It was not Ambrose's powers of eloquence that brought Augustine closer to Christ. It was his kindness.

When we show kindness, we show Christ — because He is kindness. We cannot save anyone by our kindness alone. But we can witness to the kindness that saves, and that is the Lord Jesus Christ. A compassionate, charitable kind act today can help a brother or sister love Jesus more.

The promise of the gospel in Ephesians 2:7 is this: "that in the ages to come He might show the exceeding riches of His grace in His kindness toward us in Christ Jesus."

Let us pray. Father, thank you that you are a kind God, that you have freed us from self, that we might be kind. Yet we fail in that often. So forgive us, help us to die to ourselves, and to have Christ live in us, who is kind Himself, and may that be evident in the way that we deal with one another. In Jesus' name. Amen.
